title: On-line estimation of viable cells in a hybridoma culture at various DO levels using ATP balancing and redox potential measurement
abstract:
On-Line Estimation of Viable Cells in a 
Hybridoma Culture at Various DO Levels 
Using ATP Balancing and Redox 
Potential Measurement 
Kurt Eyer" and Elmar Heinzlet 
Biological Reaction Engineering Group, Chemical Engineering Department, 
ETH Zentrum, CH-8092 Zurich, Switzerland; e-mail: 
heinzle@tech.chern. ethz.ch 
Received April 16, 1995/Accepted August 8, 1995 
Two on-line methods for the estimation of viable cell 
number in hybridoma cultivation were investigated. One 
used an empirical correlation between redox potential 
and animal cell density. The other was based on an ATP 
balance with ATP steady-state assumption. Oxygen up- 
take rate measurement provided the amount of ATP 
which was produced by oxidation of NADH. Oxygen up- 
take rate was measured either by stationary liquid phase 
balance with surface aeration or by gas balance during 
bubble aeration with headspace flushing with an inert 
gas. The amount of ATP produced through the glycolysis 
was estimated based on the amount of lactate produced. 
In cultures, in which pH was controlled via manipulation 
of the gas phase composition, the flow of CO, was linearly 
correlated with the lactate concentration. At constant dis- 
solved oxygen levels, the viable cell density was propor- 
tional to the estimated ATP production rate, during expo- 
nential growth and during later phases. The estimated 
specific ATP production rate, however, varied from 2.2 
pmol cell-' h-' at 10% air saturation to 4.5 pmol cell-' 
h-' at 100% air saturation. Specific rates of glutamine, 
glucose, and lactate followed the shape of the specific 
ATP production rate, whereas the specific oxygen uptake 
rate was minimal at around 50% air saturation. 0 1996 
John Wiley & Sons, Inc. 
Key words: cell culture on-line viable cell concentration 
ATP balance 
redox potential hybridoma - dissolved 
oxygen 
INTRODUCTION 
Viable cell number is a crucial parameter in animal cell 
cultivation. It represents the amount of active biocata- 
lyst and biological rates are usually related to viable cell 
number to get a better insight into metabolism. Viable 
cell number is also a central parameter required for 
health authorities during registration of a new process 
as well as for routine monitoring. Classical methods for 
viable cell density are off-line and use staining tech- 
niques. In principle, these allow to distinguish between 
viable and dead cells on the basis of intact or damaged 
membrane structures which either prohibit or allow the 
* Present address: Nestle R&D Center, Sonnrainstr. 19, CH-Konol- 
fingen, Switzerland. 
To whom all correspondence should be addressed. 
diffusion of dyes into the cell. Another technique ap- 
plied is flow cytometry which also uses staining to distin- 
guish between viable and dead cells but additionally 
allows the estimation of the distribution of cell charac- 
teristics of a pop~lation.'~ 
Various methods for the on-line estimation of bio- 
mass in animal cell cultivation are given in the literature. 
Merten et a1." used infrared nephelometry at 900 nm 
and were able to determine total cell numbers above 
lo5 cells mL-'. Kilburn et a1.6 used acoustic resonance 
densitometry. The detection limits were around 106 cells 
mL-'.8*9 Dielectric measurements, as described by Da- 
vey et al.,' provide viable cell numbers above lo6 cells 
mL-'. Konstantinov et al.7 described a laser turbidity 
sensor to measure total cell number. This technique 
was later applied by Zhou and Hu19 to estimate on-line 
specific rates. 
An interesting method is the estimation of biomass 
on the basis of the culture redox potential measurement 
as given by Hwang and Sinskey.' They attributed this 
correlation to the ability of cells to secrete reducing 
thiols into the surrounding medium. It is, however, 
known for a long time that dissolved oxygen (DO) and 
pH strongly influence redox potential measurement. 
Constant pH and D O  are therefore necessary precondi- 
tions for this measurement. At 90% air saturation and 
a pH of 7.25 they found an average correlation of 
In(N) = 12.86 - 0.0384 . E, where N is the cell concen- 
tration in cells mL-' and E is the redox potential given 
in millivolts. 
Another method used ATP balancing, a method gen- 
erally known for many years." Glacken et al.4 showed 
in their work that two main sources for ATP can be 
identified in mammalian cells: glycolysis with formation 
of lactate and a yield of 1 mol of ATP per mole of 
lactate produced. The second and even more important 
source is the oxidative phosphorylation in the respira- 
tory chain. With a P/O ratio of 3, 6 mol of ATP are 
produced per mole of oxygen consumed. The P/O ratio 
is usually not known with sufficient accuracy and is 
difficult to determine.l' Reported P/O values are be- 
tween 2 and 3. 
Biotechnology and Bioengineering, Vol. 49, Pp. 277-283 (1996) 
0 1996 John Wiley & Sons, Inc. 
CCC 0006-35921961030277-07 
 The direct determination of the rate of ATP produc- 
tion is very difficult.ls Because of the simultaneous pro- 
duction and consumption using various pathways, the 
analysis of the intracellular ATP concentration does not 
provide information on the rate of conversion. The 
P/O ratio and the cell yield from ATP are linearly corre- 
lated and therefore difficult to determine. Zeng et al." 
presented a method to determine both coefficients in 
a microaerobic culture. For Aerobacter aerogenes they 
found a constant value of 3.5 mol ATP per mole of 
oxygen consumed. The biomass yield from ATP varied 
with varying dilution rate. 
On the assumptions that viable cells have a constant 
specific production rate of ATP ( qATP) at constant DO 
and a P/O ratio of 3, the biomass production rate was 
calculated directly from oxygen uptake rate (ro2) and 
from the lactate production rate (rLac). 
In this work the ATP balancing and redox potential 
methods were compared with off-line measurements. 
New ways for the estimation of ro2 and rLac were estab- 
lished. To examine the possible use of other measure- 
ments for viable cell estimation, it was also studied how 
q A T p  depends on dissolved oxygen concentration and 
how it is related to other specific rates. 
MATERIALS AND METHODS 
Cell Line and Growth Medium 
HFN 7.1 (ATCC CRL 1606), a mouse hybridoma pro- 
ducing antifibronectin IgG monoclonal antibody," was 
cultured in Dulbecco's modification of Eagle's medium 
(DMEM, GIBCO/BRL AG, Basel, Switzerland) sup- 
plemented with 5% fetal calf serum (FCS, GIBCO/ 
BRL). For each experiment, a 2-mL vial was rapidly 
thawed and diluted tenfold with DMEM and was cul- 
tured via T-flasks and spinner bottles before being used 
as the inoculum for the bioreactor. The inoculum con- 
centration was approximately lo5 cells mL-'. More ex- 
perimental details are given by Eyer.2 
Cultivation System 
The cells were cultivated in a 2.4-L internal loop biore- 
actor with mechanical stirring (Bioengineering, Wald, 
Switzerland). The stirrer speed was 400 rpm. The pH 
was controlled at 7.2 and the pOz was controlled at 
constant levels. Redox potential was measured using 
platinum electrode with Ag/AgCl reference electrode 
(Model Pt-4865-35-K, Ingold, Urdorf, Switzerland). 
Calibration was made with culture medium as standard. 
The development of pH and DO control is explained 
in more detail elsewhere.2,1'.'2 The cultures were aerated 
either by surface or by bubble aeration. During surface 
aeration the total flow of N2, 02, 
COz, and air was kept 
constant at 200 mL min-' standard conditions. The mass 
transfer coefficient, KLa, was measured before each ex- 
periment (see Eyer2). For all experiments, the bioreac- 
tor was connected with a process control unit (Miinzer & 
Diehl, Overrath, Germany) and a quadrupole process 
mass spectrometer (PGM 407, Balzers, Fiirstentum 
Liechtenstein). During cell cultivation, measurements 
of C02, N2, 02, 
and air in the inlet and the outlet gas 
streams were made every 2 min. 
Analytical Procedures 
Cell concentration was determined by counting cells on 
a hemocytometer. Viability (viable cell number) was 
determined via microscopic observation subsequent to 
trypan blue staining. Glucose and lactic acid were deter- 
mined enzymatically with a YSI Model 2000 analyzer 
(Yellow Springs Instruments, Burkard Instrumente, Zu- 
rich). Glutamine and ammonia were monitored using 
commercially available enzymatic kits (Boehringer 
Mannheim GmbH, FRG). Antifibronectin antibody lev- 
els were measured by an alkaline phosphatase "sand- 
wich" ELISA technique. l 3  Further details are given 
by Eyer.2 
MASS BALANCES AND THEORY 
FOR BIOMASS ESTIMATION 
Oxygen Balances 
Oxygen uptake rate (r0J measurement in animal cell 
culture is not a simple procedure. The various methods 
and their applicability were recently investigated by 
Eyer et a1.2 The basic requirements are precise oxygen 
balances. The aeration scheme of the laboratory reactor 
used is depicted schematically in Figure 1. Either head- 
space or membrane bubble aeration could be per- 
formed. In the headspace aeration case, Go and VGB are 
both zero and transfer of oxygen and carbon dioxide 
are only via the liquid surface. 
Oxygen balances for the reactor system of Figure 1, 
assuming well mixed bubble and headspace gas and 
liquid phases, are: 
Gas phase (bubbles): 
Gas phase (headspace): 
Liquid phase: 
278

reactor operating conditions and physicochemical char-
Oxygen Uptake Rate From Stationary
The assumption of stationary gas phases was justified
because the residence time of the gas phase was only
5 min. The steady state assumption for the liquid phase
is justified by the low solubility of oxygen. These as-
sumptions lead to a simple expression for the estimation
of ro2. In the case of bubble aeration with headspace
flushing with inert gas ( yo23 = O),' the measurement
The above equation gives the estimation with the as-
sumption of equal flow rates of dry gases at the reactor
inlet and outlet. The main advantage of this method is
its independence on KLO,U and Ho2 as well as on the
measurement of dissolved oxygen which is prone to
disturbances by air bubbles not in equilibrium with the
liquid phase sticking to the electrode membrane.

Figure 2. Viable cell concentration and medium redox potential in
a hybridoma, CRL 1606, culture using 5% fetal calf serum. DO was

line measurement of redox potential and on-line estimation of viable
The main production of ATP is from the oxidation of
NADH and FADH and from glycolysis. From the litera-
ture4 and from own work2 it can be assumed that most
of the lactate is produced from glucose yielding 1 mol
of ATP per mole of lactate produced, TATp,Lac = 1. ATP
concentration in active cells is low compared with its
conversion rate. This allows for the steady-state assump-
tion (dCATpldt = O), and also means that the ATP pro-
duction rate equals the ATP consumption rate at any
time. A further assumption, originally suggested by
Glacken et a1.: is a constant ATP consumption rate per
unit amount of viable cell. The validity of this assump-
tion will be proven later experimentally. The viable cell
where Xis the viable cell concentration (cells L-'). qATp
is the specific ATP production rate (pmol cells -' h-').
rATp is the ATP production rate (pmol L-' h-'), and is
EXPERIMENTAL RESULTS AND DISCUSSION
In Figure 2 the culture redox potential, both measured
on-line within the fermentor and off-line immediately
after sampling are given with the cell concentration mea-
sured after staining with trypan blue. Both variables are
negatively correlated very much like in the results given
by Hwang and Sinskey.s Plotting the logarithm of viable
cell concentration versus the measured redox potential
(Fig. 3) gave a correlation In(N) =12.3 - 0.0175 * E,
where N is the cell concentration (cells mL-') and E is
the measured redox potential (mv). The intercept is
almost identical to the one given by Hwang and Sinskeys
at 90% air saturation and pH 7.25, whereas the slope
was only about half of that reported by them. It is obvi-
ous that each culture and medium needs its own calibra-
tion curve which can, however, easily be obtained in
one calibration batch experiment. A close inspection of
Figure 3 shows that the variations seen in this figure are
mainly between the various experiments. The deviations
are much smaller within one single experiment. This
means that the on-line method would easily follow the
shape of viable cell concentration variation but with a
In Figure 4 the flow rate of COz required to maintain
constant pH, Fco2, is plotted versus the concentration
of lactate, CLac. The data shown are from five experi-
ments and linear correlation seems well justified. In
these cultures the acid production was low enough to
permit pH control without addition of alkali.
Figure 5 depicts the results of a batch cultivation
experiment with ro2, antibody, glutamine, and cell con-
Figure 3. Correlation between viable cell concentration and medium
redox potential. Data are from five individual batch cultivations. Inoc-
ulation was about 1 . lo5 cells mL-' each. DO was controlled at 50%
air saturation, pH was 7.2 In(N) = 12.3 - 0.0175 E
BIOTECHNOLOGY AND BIOENGINEERING, VOL. 49, NO. 3, FEBRUARY 5, 1996
Figure 4. Correlation between C02 feed gas flow rate, Fc, versus
lactate concentration, C,,,. Data are from five individual batch cultiva-
tions. Inoculation was about 1 . lo5 cells ML-' each. DO was con-
trolled at 50% air saturation, pH was 7.2.
centrations. Cells were counted off-line using trypan
blue staining. Additionally, on-line estimations using
redox potential measurement and ATP balancing are
given. qATp was adjusted to give an optimal agreement
between off-line measurements using staining and on-
line measurements using ATP balancing. The agree-
ment between on-line and off-line measurements was
then very good, even after depletion of glutamine at
about 4.5 days. One might expect that at this drastic
change in metabolism would possibly change the specific
ATP production rate. Obviously, it does not.
Figure 6. Correlation of on-line with off-line measured viable cell
density. On-line estimation using redox measurement (0-full
on-line estimation using ATP balancing (@-grey
agreement. Data from Figure 5.
The question now is whether constant specific ATP
production rate of viable cells is a general characteristic.
This question can obviously not be answered on the
bases of the data presented. At low initial cell densities
(<4 x lo5 cells mL-') the estimates were significantly
different. This is likely to be caused by larger relative
errors in the determination of oxygen uptake rate, rO2,
at low culture activities but presumably primarily by
inaccuracies in viable cell counting. After glutamine
depletion ATP balancing results were significantly lower
than the other measurements. This may be caused by
the modified metabolism and by the different measure-
The on-line and off-line data are correlated with each
other as depicted in Figure 6. The correlation coefficient
of off-line measurement with ATP balancing estimation
was 0.984, and with redox-measurement it was 0.986.
The correlation between the two completely indepen-
dent on-line methods was 0.995. From this graph it is
even more clear that the on-line estimations are gener-
ally in agreement with off-line viable cell counting. Simi-
lar results were obtained in a whole series of experi-
ments with various controlled DO and pH values.
It was observed that the specific ATP production rate,
qATp, estimated on-line varied when the concentration
of DO was changed (Fig. 7). The values of qATp below
Figure 5. Time course of ro2, glutamine, product, and viable cell
concentration in a hybridoma batch culture. DO was controlled at
50% air saturation, pH was 7.2. Viable cell count with trypan blue
on-line estimation of viable cell count using ATP balanc-
); and on-line estimation of viable cell count using redox
measurement and empirical correlation (-).
Figure 7. Dependency of specific ATP production rate, APR,, on
dissolved oxygen concentration, DO, in batch culture.
EYER AND HEINZLE: CELL CULTURE ON-LINE VIABLE CELLS AND ATP
20% air saturation have to be interpreted with care,
because the estimation of ro2 was less accurate at these
low values of DO. The reason for this are difficulties
with control of DO at these low DO values. One can
still see qATP increase with increasing DO. This does,
however, not result in a correspondingly increasing cell
concentration. For this cell line the final cell and product
concentrations were maximal at about 50% air satura-
tion.2 For the on-line cell estimation this means that the
coefficient, qATp, of Eq. (12) depends on DO.
It is of interest to question whether viable cells could
possibly be estimated solely based on the measurement
of glucose and glutamine consumption. This would be
possible if glucose was quantitatively converted into
lactate and if the ratio of glutamine oxidation and its
conversion to biomass were constant or known. This
was studied by estimating the specific rates for glucose,
glutamine, lactate, and oxygen conversion. Figure 8A
depicts the specific glucose uptake ( qcluc) and the spe-
cific lactate production rates ( qLac) at various levels of
controlled DO. A quantitative conversion of glucose
consumed into lactate would make the curves coincide.
It is obvious that, at very low DO, the lactate produc-
tion is even larger than the maximum expected produc-
tion from glucose. This can be explained such that lac-
tate was also produced from glutamine or other sources.
Above 10% air saturation qLac was always lower than
expected from quantitative conversion of glucose into
Figure 8. Specific rates for glucose, lactate, and glutamine at various
DO levels. (A) Glucose uptake, qcluc and lactate production, qLdC;
(B) glutamine uptake, qGlut, and oxygen uptake, qO,; (C) yield of
lactate from glucose, YL,<;. The dashed line in (C) indicates quantita-
tive conversion of glucose into lactate.
lactate. This means that part of the glucose was either
oxidized or converted to cellular material. In Figure 8C
the lactate-glucose yield coefficient is plotted versus
DO. At 1% air saturation more lactate was produced
than was possible from glucose alone (YLiG > 2). A min-
imum was reached at about 20% saturation. Above 50%
saturation YLic decreased slightly. At 100% DO about
40% of the glucose was not converted to lactate. The
consequences from this are that glucose uptake rate
should not be used for the estimation of qATp. The spe-
cific glutamine uptake rate, qGlut, depended also on the
Again a minimum was observed at about 10% air
saturation (Fig. 7B). qGlut was increasing more than qGluc
and qLac. The maximal oxygen uptake rate of the whole
was constant over the whole range of
DO (rOzmax = 400 pmol L-’ h-’ at cell concentrations
around 2 X lo6 cells ML-’). The maximum cell concen-
tration obtained in various batches varied significantly
having a maximum at 50% air saturation.* Because of
the varying viable cell concentration, the specific oxygen
uptake rate at maximum cell concentration ( qo2max)
minimal at 50% air saturation and increased toward
lower and higher DO values. The reason for this finding
is not yet well understood. A possible explanation is
that cells not detected as viable were consuming oxygen.
Viable cell concentration was estimated on-line using
two methods. One method, already described earlier by
Hwang and Sinskey’ for various cell lines, used the re-
dox potential measurement of the medium using a plati-
num electrode and empirical correlation of the cell con-
centration with the redox potential. The other method,
adapted from Glacken et a].: uses ATP balancing. Here
ro2 was estimated with two different methods using sta-
tionary liquid and stationary gas phase balancing. The
concentration of lactate, CLac, was estimated from Fco2,
the flow of carbon dioxide to the reactor, which was
manipulated to control the pH of the reactor.
These methods can easily be implemented provided
that redox potential, ro2, and Fco2 are measured. If no
more carbon dioxide is present in the feed gas, alkali
has to be added to keep pH constant. Then the alkali
consumption can be used for the estimation of rLac as
was done by Glacken et aL4 The specific ATP produc-
tion rate, qATp, was a function of DO. This would compli-
cate the on-line analysis when DO is varying signifi-
cantly. With biomass, rO2, and CLac estimated on-line,
corresponding specific rates can easily be estimated on-
line as has been reported by Zhou and Hu.lY It does
not seem possible to base the on-line estimation of via-
ble cells using ATP balancing on measurements other
